title: Roast turkey
subtitle: The Christmas centrepiece, unstuffed and reliably cooked
excerpt: A whole turkey roasted unstuffed, with butter under the skin and bacon draped over the breast. The unstuffed method is faster and more reliable: no guessing whether the stuffing cavity has reached a safe temperature. A 5 kg bird feeds ten to twelve people and takes about 3 hours at 180°C.
sourceNotes: The turkey is the standard British Christmas centrepiece, a position it has held since the 19th century. The main challenge is size: a bird large enough to feed a Christmas gathering can take 4 to 5 hours to cook through, and the breast dries out long before the thighs are done. Bacon over the breast, butter under the skin, and not stuffing the main cavity all address this problem.

content.text: A 5 kg bird feeds 10 to 12 people. Allow 30 to 35 minutes per kg at 180°C for an unstuffed bird. A meat thermometer is essential: 75°C in the thickest part of the thigh, away from the bone. The breast dries out faster than the thigh, bacon draped over the breast and butter pushed under the skin both protect it.

Rest time matters more with turkey than with any other roast because of the size. 40 minutes under foil allows the internal temperature to even out across a large bird. The turkey will still be hot enough to carve after 40 minutes, cover well with a double layer of foil and a thick tea towel.

items.name: Streaky bacon
items.prepNote: streaky rashers, to drape over the breast

content.text: Remove the {{turkey-whole}} from the fridge 2 hours before roasting. Heat the oven to 180°C. Pat the turkey dry inside and out. Loosen the skin over both breasts with your fingers and push half the {{unsalted-butter}} underneath, distributing it evenly. Rub the remaining butter all over the outside. Season generously with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Push the {{lemon}}, {{onion}}, and {{thyme}} into the main cavity. Place breast-side up in a large roasting tin. Drape the {{streaky-bacon}} rashers over the breast in overlapping rows. Roast at 180°C for 3 to 3.5 hours (30 to 35 minutes per kg), until a thermometer reads 75°C in the thickest part of the thigh. Remove the bacon for the final 30 minutes to allow the breast skin to colour. Rest for 40 minutes under two layers of foil and a tea towel before carving.

items.fix: Check the thigh temperature rather than timing alone. A 75°C thigh means the breast is already past optimal. For very large birds, tent with foil earlier and remove only in the last 30 minutes
items.cause: Overcooked, or bird too large for the oven to cook evenly
items.symptom: Breast dry despite butter and bacon

items.fix: Remove the bacon 30 to 40 minutes before the bird is done, not 15 minutes. A very large cold bird drops oven temperature significantly when it goes in, factor this into timing
items.cause: Bacon removed too late, or oven temperature dropped when a cold turkey went in
items.symptom: Thigh temperature correct but breast skin pale and uncoloured

items.fix: A 5 kg bird needs 24 to 36 hours in the fridge to defrost completely. If in doubt, check that the cavity is completely clear of ice and the legs flex freely
items.cause: Insufficient defrost time in the fridge
items.symptom: Bird not fully defrosted in the centre

content.text: The Christmas turkey is the most culturally loaded roast in the British calendar. It requires more planning than any other roast: defrost time, oven size, serving time, resting space. The actual cooking technique is straightforward once you have the temperature right. The anxiety is almost entirely logistical, not culinary.
